title: Module 1: Signals
text:
A 28-year-old woman comes to your clinic with episodes of “staring off” lasting 10–15 seconds, happening several times a day. Her partner says she doesn’t respond during these episodes. You order an EEG. The technician hands you a tracing. It looks like this:
Can you tell what’s happening? When does the abnormal activity start? What’s different about it?
By the end of this tutorial, you’ll be able to answer those questions — and understand why the signal looks the way it does. But first, we need to learn the language that describes any electrical signal from the body.

title: Module 1: Signals
text:
The Basics
Every cell in your body produces tiny electrical signals. Neurons fire. Heart muscle contracts. Skeletal muscle twitches. These are not abstract physics — these are your patient’s physiology, translated into voltage over time.
A signal is just a measurement that changes over time. When we record an EEG, ECG, or EMG, we’re measuring voltage (in microvolts or millivolts) at each moment.
The simplest signal you can imagine is a smooth, repeating wave. It has two properties that matter right now:

Amplitude: How tall the wave is. Bigger amplitude = stronger signal.
Frequency: How fast the wave repeats. Higher frequency = more cycles per second.

That’s it. Two knobs. Let’s play with them.

title: Module 1: Signals
text:
Interactive: Build a Signal
Drag the sliders. Watch the wave change in real time.

  
    Try a clinical preset:
    
      Alpha (10 Hz)
    
    
      Delta (2 Hz)
    
    
      Beta (20 Hz)
    
    
      Heart (~72 bpm)
    
  
  
  
    Amplitude (μV):
      5.0
    
    
  
  
    Frequency (Hz):
      10.0
    
    
  
  




Experiment:

Set the frequency to 10 Hz. This is roughly the speed of the alpha rhythm — the dominant EEG wave when you close your eyes.
Now crank it to 1 Hz. This is the delta range — slow waves you see in deep sleep.
Set amplitude to 2 μV — that’s a weak EEG signal. Now set it to 10 μV — stronger. In real EEG, amplitudes are typically 10–100 μV.

title: Module 1: Signals
text:
One More Thing: Phase
You now know amplitude (how tall) and frequency (how fast). There’s one more property worth seeing — phase.
Phase tells you where in the cycle the wave is at a given moment. Two signals can have the same frequency and amplitude but be offset from each other — like two runners on the same track, one slightly ahead of the other.
Drag the slider below. Both waves have the same frequency and amplitude — only the phase changes.

  
    Phase offset:
      0°
    
    
  
  
  
    Both waves are identical. Try shifting the purple wave by 180° — now they're perfectly out of sync.
  




Phase won’t come up much in this tutorial — but remember this slider. When we get to EEG localization later, neurologists use phase reversal (where one electrode’s signal flips relative to its neighbor) to pinpoint exactly where a seizure starts. Same concept, real clinical stakes.

title: Module 1: Signals
text:
Why Does This Matter Clinically?
Different brain states produce different frequencies and amplitudes:









Brain State
Dominant Frequency
Typical Amplitude
What’s Happening




Deep sleep
1–4 Hz (delta)
100–200 μV
Large populations of neurons firing in sync


Relaxed, eyes closed
8–13 Hz (alpha)
30–50 μV
Thalamo-cortical idle rhythm


Focused attention
13–30 Hz (beta)
10–20 μV
Cortical neurons busy with different tasks


Active thinking
>30 Hz (gamma)
5–10 μV
Local, fast processing



Notice the pattern: slower rhythms tend to be bigger, faster rhythms tend to be smaller. This isn’t a coincidence — slow rhythms reflect more neurons firing together (synchrony), which sums to a larger voltage at the scalp.

Amplitude tells you how much electrical activity is reaching the electrode. Frequency tells you how fast that activity is oscillating. Together, they are the first two things you assess when you look at any physiological recording — EEG, ECG, or EMG.

title: Module 1: Signals
text:
It’s Not Just the Brain
The same two properties — amplitude and frequency — describe signals from other parts of the body:









Signal
Typical Amplitude
Typical Frequency
Source




EEG (brain)
10–100 μV
1–100 Hz
Cortical pyramidal neurons


ECG (heart)
0.5–3 mV
~1 Hz (60 bpm)
Cardiac muscle depolarization


EMG (muscle)
0.1–5 mV
20–500 Hz
Motor unit action potentials



The ECG is about 1,000× larger than the EEG. The EMG has much higher frequencies than either. These differences aren’t trivia — they determine what equipment you need, what filters you set, and what artifacts you worry about. (More on artifacts in Module 6.)

title: Module 1: Signals
section: Compare: EEG vs ECG vs EMG
text:
Compare: EEG vs ECG vs EMG
What do these three signals actually look like side by side? Same 2 seconds of time, three different parts of the body. Notice the amplitude scales — they’re wildly different.

  
  
    EEG (top) — ~50 μV. Alpha rhythm at 10 Hz. This is what you see over the occipital cortex, eyes closed. Tiny signal.
    ECG (middle) — ~1 mV. The tall spike is the QRS complex — ventricular depolarization. That's 1000× larger than EEG.
    EMG (bottom) — bursts during contraction, quiet at rest. Much faster oscillations — motor units firing asynchronously.

title: Module 1: Signals
text:
Quiz: Check Your Understanding

  Quiz

  
    Q1: A signal has a larger amplitude. What does that tell you?
    
       A. It means the signal is faster
    
    
       B. The voltage is higher — more electrical activity is reaching the electrode
    
    
       C. The electrode is closer to the source
    
    
       D. The patient is awake
    
    
  

  

  
    Q2: A healthy resting heart rate is about 70 beats per minute. What is the frequency of the ECG signal?
    
       A. 0.5 Hz — about one beat every two seconds
    
    
       B. About 1.2 Hz — roughly 70 beats per minute
    
    
       C. 10 Hz — same as EEG alpha rhythm
    
    
       D. 60 Hz — one beat per second
    
    
  

  

  
    Q3: Slow EEG rhythms (delta, during sleep) have much larger amplitude than fast rhythms (gamma). Why?
    
       A. More neurons are firing at higher frequency
    
    
       B. More neurons are firing in synchrony, so their voltages add up
    
    
       C. The electrodes are more sensitive to slow signals
    
    
       D. The skull filters out fast signals more than slow ones

title: Module 1: Signals
text:
Summary
You now know two things about any physiological signal:

Amplitude — how tall the wave is (measured in μV for EEG, mV for ECG/EMG)
Frequency — how many cycles per second (measured in Hz)

In the next module, we’ll tackle a fundamental question: how does a machine capture a continuously changing biological signal and turn it into numbers a computer can work with?
(Spoiler: it can go wrong in interesting ways.)

← Home Module 2: Sampling →

title: EEG & Signal Processing
text:
You’re training to become a doctor. At some point — maybe in neurology, maybe in the ICU, maybe in the OR — you will look at a screen showing electrical signals from a patient’s brain, heart, or muscles.
This tutorial will teach you to read those signals. Not just glance at them — actually understand what you’re seeing.

title: EEG & Signal Processing
text:
You’re training to become a doctor. At some point — maybe in neurology, maybe in the ICU, maybe in the OR — you will look at a screen showing electrical signals from a patient’s brain, heart, or muscles.
This tutorial will teach you to read those signals. Not just glance at them — actually understand what you’re seeing.

title: EEG & Signal Processing
text:
How This Works
Each module is interactive. You’ll drag sliders, change parameters, and watch signals respond in real time. There are short quiz questions along the way — these are not graded. They’re just checkpoints to make sure the concepts are landing.
Module 8 is graded. That’s where you’ll get real EEG data and find out what’s going on with the patient.

title: EEG & Signal Processing
text:
Modules



#
Module
What You’ll Learn




1
Signals
What electrical signals from the body look like and how to describe them


2
Sampling
How we capture continuous biological signals with a digital machine


3
Filtering
How to clean up a noisy recording to see what matters


4
FFT
How to reveal the hidden frequencies in a signal


5
EEG Basics
What the EEG measures, where electrodes go, and what the bands mean


6
Artifacts
Telling brain activity apart from eye blinks, muscle, and noise


7
Spectrograms
Watching brain rhythms change over time


8
The Challenge
A real EEG recording. A real clinical question. Your job to figure it out.

title: EEG & Signal Processing
text:
What You’ll Need

A web browser (you’re using one right now — good start)
For Module 8: Python or MATLAB, and a willingness to ask an AI for help writing code

Let’s begin.
